note: Layer 3 = the 'sliced' mode: time-slice the WRITE pass. Producer only buffers the latest intended value per cell (cheap, coalesced/drop-stale); a budgeted, input-reserved, cursor-fair loop applies a slice of writes to state AND paints them each frame. Goal: no single long task in the producer->state->pixel pipeline. dot=0, 20x.

sliced_dashboard_worstBlock_vs_cells_ren.note: worstBlock scales with TOTAL cells (not churn) — but see the profile: under CPU throttle the Long Tasks wall-clock is inflated by throttle pauses, so worstBlock is NOT a clean CPU-blocking metric here. Use input-delay (measured directly) instead.

sliced_dashboard_3000_instrumented.note: maxProducer / maxSlice are the actual synchronous durations of the producer pass and the slice loop (incl. its batched flush). Both are cheap and budget-respecting — the chunking WORKS at the JS level. The remaining wall-clock 'worstBlock' is throttle inflation, not a real 2s task.

cpu_profile_sliced_dashboard_3000.note: CDP CPU profile, 20x, 5s. Self-time % (excluding 44% '(program)' throttle overhead). The dominant REAL cost is the Lume kernel write path, even with ZERO subscribers (sliced renders via pull, no effects).

findings.chunking_works: sliced keeps the producer (7ms) and slice (16ms) budget-bounded per frame. The write pass IS chunkable.
findings.real_floor_is_the_kernel_write_path: Writing N reactive values costs O(N) through set + notifySubscribers + flushBatchedStates (~27% of active CPU). Chunking spreads it across frames but does not reduce the total; at 3000 cells the per-frame slice still writes enough that input (~1155ms) is no better than pull/off.
findings.notifySubscribers_no_sub_waste: notifySubscribers (state.js:131) does Array.from(pendingNotifications) + iterate on every flush even when a store has NO subscribers. The pull/sliced pattern (reactive state, no effects) pays this for nothing. A no-subscriber fast-path in the kernel would cut it — concrete, shippable core optimization.
findings.truest_pull_bypasses_reactive_state: Since a pull renderer has no subscribers, the high-churn layer could hold PLAIN data (not Lume state) and skip set/notify/flush entirely — zero kernel write cost. The sliced experiment writes Lume state unnecessarily.
findings.worstBlock_under_throttle_unreliable: CDP CPU throttling inflates Long Tasks wall-clock with inserted pauses, so worstBlock >> CPU time. Relative comparisons under the same throttle are still directionally valid; absolute ms are not. Input-delay (event.timeStamp based) is the clean metric.
